title: 'Hustlers' Is Undeniable and So Is Jennifer Lopez, Oscar Contender
format: Sean Fennessey and Amanda Dobbins walk Jennifer Lopez's career the week Hustlers opens, from the four-films-in-1997 arrival through the bad rom-coms and the Bennifer wipeout to a decade away and the comeback. Plus: Sean talks to Hustlers writer-director Lorene Scafaria.

segments.heading: The Jennifer Lopez career arc
segments.note: her breakthrough, her pinnacle, then years away, with Hustlers as the comeback

films.title: Money Train
films.note: The crime caper with Woody Harrelson and Wesley Snipes; her first big supporting role, before megastardom.

films.title: Blood and Wine
films.note: Bob Rafelson's steamy thriller with Jack Nicholson. She plays the femme fatale, a role she got cast in a lot in this period. Amanda watched her tango with Nicholson the night before.

films.title: Selena
films.note: The best-remembered of her films and, they agree, a total-movie-star turn. Amanda calls the film itself maybe the most hagiographic biopic she's ever seen, but Lopez is riveting, and the stage presence is there from the start, nothing borrowed.

films.title: Anaconda
films.note: An all-time good-bad movie. She's perfectly credible fighting a giant snake in an unfortunate khaki vest.

films.title: U Turn
films.note: Oliver Stone's noir blow-up. Sean rewatched it; her character is the most retrograde man-manipulating part, and it doesn't quite work, but four wildly different films in one year signaled an arrival.

films.title: Out of Sight
films.note: Both hosts' personal pinnacle. As Karen Sisco she's witty and in control, always one step ahead of George Clooney, but the trunk scene and the snow-globe softness are what make it: a slightly reserved performance that doesn't swing for the fences.

films.title: The Cell
films.note: Tarsem's beautiful, self-indulgent showcase with none of the essence of Lopez in it. Sean got it as one of his first two DVDs alongside The Matrix and has seen it a lot anyway.

films.title: The Wedding Planner
films.note: Not a good romantic comedy. She and Matthew McConaughey have no chemistry, she's buttoned up and not funny, and she's cast as an Italian American rather than allowed to be Jennifer Lopez.

films.title: Angel Eyes
films.note: One of the intense thriller/crime pivots she made between rom-coms.

films.title: Enough
films.note: The other crime/thriller swing of the era, paired with Angel Eyes.

films.title: Maid in Manhattan
films.note: A bland commercial pivot directed, oddly, by Wayne Wang. Shea Serrano and Amanda once wrote a 'would this get made today?' piece about its assumptions.

films.title: Gigli
films.note: Branded the biggest disaster in movie history; it's not, it's just bad. Made $7.2M against a $75.6M budget despite pairing J.Lo and Ben Affleck at their most famous, Martin Brest directing, Al Pacino, and cinematographer Robert Elswit.

films.title: Jersey Girl
films.note: Kevin Smith's self-conscious commercial swing, shot by Vilmos Zsigmond, also with Affleck. She plays the wife who dies early. The Gigli-into-Jersey Girl one-two radically changed her career.

films.title: Shall We Dance
films.note: She's a dance instructor in a strictly-ballroom-but-not-campy setup. Amanda hasn't seen it; Sean has a problem with movie titles that end in question marks.

films.title: Monster-in-Law
films.note: One of the titles they flag a listener will accuse them of ignoring.

films.title: Bordertown
films.note: Barely seen; part of the retreat after the Bennifer run.

films.title: El Cantante
films.note: The Hector Lavoe biopic with then-husband Marc Anthony. Not very well seen.

films.title: The Back-up Plan
films.note: One of the long-forgotten later films; part of what they call a roughly 10-year absence as a credible movie star.

films.title: What to Expect When You're Expecting
films.note: Another of the forgotten ensemble titles from the wilderness decade.

films.title: Parker
films.note: Grouped with the other quickly-forgotten films of the 2010s.

films.title: Second Act
films.note: A working-girl dramedy Amanda wanted to watch but couldn't rent. Made for $16M and grossed $72M; you don't quite believe J.Lo as someone struggling.

segments.heading: In conversation
segments.note: Sean and the writer-director on adapting the real story, casting Lopez, and mid-budget movies

films.title: Hustlers
films.note: She took the Jessica Pressler article as a writing assignment after The Meddler, wrote maybe 20 drafts through a studio change to STX, and treated the pole work like a sports movie. Scorsese was circling to direct; Adam McKay told her he hoped Scorsese passed. She calls Lopez the reason it got made, and by far the best thing she's done since Out of Sight. One of the real subjects, the one Lopez plays, is suing.

films.title: The Meddler
films.note: Her personal previous film about her mother and her father's death; she wanted to swing the pendulum away from something that personal.

films.title: Seeking a Friend for the End of the World
films.note: Her ambitious debut feature; she didn't go to film school, came up directing theater and shorts.

films.title: Once Upon a Time in Hollywood
films.note: Her answer to the closing 'last great thing' question. She found it moving and human, with a restraint she liked, which is why she's praying character movies aren't a death rattle.

films: What Ever Happened to Baby Jane?
